original_abstract:
In our ongoing search for novel antidiabetic agents, we have designed and successfully synthesized a library of new bis-Schiff base derivatives featuring a thiobarbituric acid scaffold. These compounds were synthesized through a multi-step pathway and structurally characterized using high-resolution spectroscopic techniques. The entire series was evaluated for in vitro inhibitory potential against the α-glucosidase enzyme to assess its therapeutic relevance. Notably, compounds 8 ($IC_{50} = 0.10 \pm 0.05$ µM) and 9 ($IC_{50} = 0.13 \pm 0.03$ µM) emerged as the most potent inhibitors, outperforming the clinical standard acarbose ($IC_{50} = 0.27 \pm 0.04$ µM). Furthermore, derivatives 5, 6, 7, 10, and 4 demonstrated significant to strong inhibitory profiles, with $IC_{50}$ values ranging from $0.32 \pm 0.03$ to $0.52 \pm 0.02$ µM. 

To explore the molecular basis of this activity, docking simulations were performed with the target protein, identifying favorable binding affinities and key stabilizing interactions within the active site. The electronic and chemical characteristics of these molecules were further investigated using density functional theory (DFT) at the B3LYP/6-311++G(d,p) level. Intramolecular hydrogen bonding was characterized using DFT-D3 and reduced density gradient (RDG) analysis. Additionally, time-dependent DFT (TD-DFT) calculations at the CAM-B3LYP/6-311++G(d,p) level were utilized to evaluate electronic reactivity parameters, providing a comprehensive description of the compounds' structural and functional properties.
humanized_abstract:
In continuation of our efforts to discover new synthetic hypoglycemic drugs, a new group of bis-Schiff base compounds with thiobarbituric acid nucleus were synthesized by multi steps from different aromatic aldehydes. The structures were supported by several spectral methods such as 1 H, 13 C NMR, MS and FT-IR. All synthesized compounds were evaluated for inhibitory activities against α-glucosidase using a colorimetric assay and the IC50 values were determined. Among them, compound 8 had the highest inhibitory activity toward α-glucosidase with an IC50 value of 0.10 ± 0.05 µM and was even more potent than the commonly used drug acarbose with an IC50 value of 0.27 ± 0.04 µM, whereas compounds 9 and 5, 6, 7, 10, 4 also possessed significant to strong inhibitory activities toward α-glucosidase with IC50 values ranging from 0.32 ± 0.03 to 0.52 ± 0.02 µM.

To investigate the molecular mechanism of the activity of a compound or derivatives as modulators of protein function, particularly as potential activators of BCL2 to selectively induce death of preapoptotic BCL2 family proteins bearing a BH3-only domain and/or other apoptosis-restricted proteins through the binding to specific sites of the target protein by means of Docking Studies to investigate binding affinity on target protein and molecular interactions at the active site level. In order to provide the electron density and an understanding of the chemical behavior of the molecules, they were optimized with the aid of the Density Functional Theory (DFT) at the B3LYP/6-311++G (d, p) level. Hydrogen bonding within the molecules were further studied and confirmed with DFT-D3 and Reduced Density Gradient (RDG) analysis. On the other hand, Time Dependent DFT (TD-DFT) at the CAM-B3LYP/6-311++G (d, p) level were employed to investigate the electronic reactivity parameters such as Energy and Intensity for absorption and emission transitions which provide further information on the molecular structure and biological function of compounds of interest.
